Composite manuscript, contains Sermons; patristic and classical excerpts; proverbs; computational table; Latin songs.

According to DIAMM: 
Origin: England
Owner: Lyre abbey
Copied by several hands; material is arranged with distinct layouts and inconsistent quire numbering. May be connected to English dependencies of Lyre Abbey (notes of English names and a colophon recorded travel to England in 1246)

- Planctus ante nescia
- Licet eger cum egrotis
- Est tonus sic, ut, re, ut, aut re, mi, re

Ar Salvatoris hodie sanguis pregustatur

Av De nature fracto iure

113r-v (=Cr-v) Flos de spina procreatur

Das zweispaltige Doppelblatt, das nicht das innerste Doppelblatt einer Lage bildet, überliefert neben 26 Walther-Strophen auch eine vollständige (Palästinalied) und drei fragmentarische Strophen-Melodien (zu Cormeau 115; zweiter Stollen des König-Friedrich-Tones; erster Stollen des 2. Philippstones) in gotischer deutscher Choralnotation und bildet damit die "wichtigste Quelle für Melodien Walthers" (Horst Brunner).

Origin: France ?_x000D_
Bifolio with music pieces added to an incomplete copy of the Historia scholastica by Petrus Comestor, chancellor of Notre-Dame of Paris in the 1160s. Contains four monophonic conducti and one two-part motet, all belonging to the Notre-Dame repertoire. Most of the texts have medieval attribution to Philippe the Chancellor (d. 1236) in other sources. The manuscript does not seem to have originated in Spain. In fact, both handwritings do not display Spanish features and are rather closer to French scripts of the 13th century._x000D_
See description in https://www.diamm.ac.uk/sources/3967/#/_x000D_
Bevilacque:  "messine neumes". Note CC: ?? The axis of the notation and the shapes of the neumes are not at all lotharingian.

f-III-18 doesn't have this item on f. 179 (we have digital pictures of fol. 170-180). HS writes "Escorial, f.III.18, s. 14, fol. 179r. In Kurzzeilen geschr. Str. 2b fehlt". I've checked  f-III-18 as an old shelfmark, but the current shelfmark corresponding to the old f-III-18, d-III-3, doesn't have the piece either. I've browsed all the catalogues of the Escorial and couldn't find any match for the incipit Aristipe, for a fol. 179 with rhyme verses etc. I've checked the Greek ms. too. Couldn't find anything...
